deepseek-r1-distill-llama-8b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this patient.
Let's go through the information step by step.
The patient is a 27-year-old man who presented with diarrhea, fatigue, and eosinophilia.
He had been in good health until two weeks before admission when fatigue started.
He recently moved from Indonesia to the US, and after arriving, he developed bloating and loose, non-bloody stools.
His symptoms of diarrhea persisted, and his exercise tolerance dropped.
He was seen at a health center and then referred to the hospital.
Looking at his past medical history, two years ago, he had similar symptoms: intermittent diarrhea, abdominal cramping, tenesmus, nausea, and weight loss of 9 kg.
Stool analysis showed microscopic blood, and a colonoscopy confirmed ulcerative colitis, which was treated with mesalamine.
His blood tests at that time and 7 months ago were normal.
He's currently on mesalamine and acetaminophen.
He has no allergies, is a vegetarian, drinks alcohol occasionally, has a remote smoking history, and no drug use.
He's from India, lived in Indonesia and Australia, and recently traveled to a rural area in India.
He's monogamous, no kids, and moved to the US for school.
No family history of cancer or autoimmune diseases.
On exam, his vital signs are normal.
He has a palpable spleen tip and an erythematous papular rash on his anterior chest.
Other exam findings are normal.
Lab work shows:
- Normal creatinine, magnesium, bilirubin, amylase, lipase, uric acid, fibrinogen, CK, vitamin B12, tryptase.
- INR and D-dimer are normal, so no clotting issues.
- Positive for total anti-HAV, negative for anti-HCV, anti-HBc, and HBsAg.
So, he has hepatitis A antibodies but not the virus itself.
- Other tests (urinalysis, chest X-ray, CT abdomen/pelvis) are normal except for enlarged mesenteric and retroperitoneal lymph nodes on CT, up to 3 cm.
The PET scan shows FDG uptake in the skeleton and spleen, but not in the lymph nodes.
A biopsy of a retroperitoneal lymph node showed reactive hyperplasia with increased eosinophils and plasma cells, no lymphoma.
Stool tests are negative for C. diff, protozoa, helminths, and enteric pathogens.
No antibodies to strongyloides, toxocara, trichinella, or HIV.
No CMV detected.
Cultures are sterile.
So, putting this together: He has a history of ulcerative colitis, which was treated successfully before.
Now, he's presenting with similar symptoms but with new findings.
The positive anti-HAV suggests recent hepatitis A infection.
The eosinophilia is notable.
The CT and PET show enlarged lymph nodes, and the biopsy shows reactive hyperplasia with eosinophils and plasma cells.
Hepatitis A can cause systemic symptoms like fatigue and diarrhea.
The rash could be a result of the viral infection.
The eosinophilia might be due to the body's response to the infection.
The enlarged lymph nodes could be reactive due to the immune response or a secondary effect of the viral infection.
The absence of other pathogens and the negative tests for other conditions like HIV, CMV, and parasites makes me think it's not an opportunistic infection.
The fact that the stool tests are negative for typical pathogens and C. diff rules out those causes.
The history of ulcerative colitis might make him more susceptible, but the current presentation aligns more with a viral hepatitis presentation.
The PET scan showing FDG uptake in the skeleton could indicate systemic inflammation, which is common in viral hepatitis.
The lack of lymph node FDG uptake in the nodes but high in the spleen and skeleton suggests the infection is more systemic but not involving the lymph nodes extensively.
So, putting it all together, the most likely diagnosis is Acute Hepatitis A.
